title: Indices and Market Benchmarks
objectives: Explain what a stock market index is and why it exists
Identify the major NZ and global indices and what they track
Interpret an index movement — what a 1.5% rise in the NZX 50 means

vocabulary.definition: A basket of selected shares used to represent the overall performance of a market or sector. The NZX 50 tracks NZ's 50 largest listed companies.
New Zealand's main share market index, tracking the 50 largest companies listed on the NZX by market cap. Used as the benchmark for NZ share market performance.
An index tracking the 500 largest listed companies in the United States. The most widely used benchmark for US (and global) share market performance.
A standard against which investment performance is measured. If a fund manager's portfolio returned 8% and the NZX 50 returned 10%, the fund underperformed its benchmark.
A fund that automatically buys all shares in an index in proportion to their weight. Low cost, broadly diversified. The most common investment vehicle for ordinary investors.

activities.title: What Is a Stock Market Index?
activities.notePrompt: Write: Why do we need an index? What problem does it solve compared to tracking individual share prices?
activities.content:
The NZX lists around 200 companies. Every day, some go up and some go down. To understand whether 'the market' as a whole moved up or down, you need an index — a summary measure.

An index is a basket of selected shares that represents a market or sector. The price changes of all shares in the basket are combined (weighted by company size) into a single number that changes throughout the day.

When a news report says 'the NZX 50 rose 1.2% today,' it means the weighted average of NZ's 50 largest listed companies rose 1.2% — not that every share went up. Some companies in the index might have fallen while the overall index rose.

Indices also serve as benchmarks. Fund managers are assessed on whether their portfolios beat the index. An investor who earns 6% while the NZX 50 returns 9% has underperformed the benchmark — they would have been better off in an index fund.

Why are indices useful for ordinary investors? They provide a simple, daily answer to 'how is the market doing?' without requiring you to track hundreds of individual shares.

activities.title: Major Indices You Should Know
activities.notePrompt: Create a reference table: Index | Country | Number of companies | What it represents. Fill in all five indices from this slide.
activities.content:
Here are the indices most relevant to NZ investors.

NZX 50: New Zealand's benchmark. Tracks NZ's 50 largest listed companies. When you hear 'the NZ market was up today,' this is the index being referenced. Major components include Fisher and Paykel Healthcare, Mainfreight, Meridian Energy, Spark, and Infratil.

S&P 500 (USA): The world's most tracked index. 500 large US companies. Dominated by technology — Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet (Google), and Meta make up around 25% of the index by weight. Considered the best proxy for overall global equity market performance.

Dow Jones Industrial Average (USA): 30 major US companies. Older and less comprehensive than the S&P 500. Still widely reported in news because of its long history (founded 1896).

NASDAQ Composite (USA): Over 3,000 companies, heavily weighted toward technology. Follows companies like Tesla, Netflix, and Nvidia. More volatile than the S&P 500.

ASX 200 (Australia): Australia's 200 largest listed companies. Closely followed in NZ — many NZ investors hold Australian shares and the Australian economy is closely tied to NZ's.

When a US journalist says 'the market was down today,' they almost always mean the S&P 500.

activities.statement: If the NZX 50 index rises 2% in a day, it means every company in the index rose in price that day.
activities.explanation: False. An index is a weighted average. A 2% rise means the weighted average of all 50 companies rose 2% — but individual companies could have fallen significantly while the index still rose, if the larger companies (which have more weight in the index) gained. For example, if Fisher and Paykel Healthcare (a major index component) surged 5%, the index could rise even if many smaller index companies fell. This is why an index is a summary measure, not a statement about every individual stock.

activities.title: Index Funds — The Smart Way Most People Invest
activities.notePrompt: Write: What is an index fund? Why might it outperform a fund managed by professional stock pickers over 10 years?
activities.content:
An index fund is a fund that automatically replicates an index. It buys every share in the index in proportion to its weight, holding them without active stock selection.

Why index funds are popular:
— Low cost: no expensive fund managers required. Index funds typically charge 0.1% to 0.5% per year. Actively managed funds charge 0.8% to 1.5% or more.
— Diversification: you own all major companies in the market, not a concentrated bet on a few.
— Performance: academic research consistently shows most active fund managers fail to beat their benchmark index over 10+ years. The S&P 500 outperforms around 85% to 90% of US fund managers over 15 years.

NZ examples of index funds and low-cost providers:
— Simplicity NZ Share Fund — tracks the NZX 50 at very low cost
— Smartshares NZX 50 ETF (FNZ) — an Exchange Traded Fund tracking the NZX 50, buying shares in it like a regular share
— Vanguard index funds — the world's largest index fund provider (US-based), accessible to NZ investors through platforms like InvestNow

For most people investing for the long run, a low-cost index fund is the recommended starting point. Warren Buffett has publicly instructed his estate to invest in index funds after his death.

questions.question: A fund manager's portfolio returned 7.5% last year. The NZX 50 index returned 9.8% over the same period. What does this mean for the fund manager?
options: The fund manager performed well — 7.5% is a positive return
The fund manager underperformed the benchmark by 2.3 percentage points — investors would have been better off in a low-cost NZX 50 index fund
The fund manager outperformed because active management generated higher quality returns than passive indexing
The comparison is invalid — fund returns cannot be compared to index returns
questions.explanation: When a fund manager underperforms the benchmark index, investors would have been better off — and paid less in fees — by investing in a simple index fund. 7.5% vs 9.8% is a 2.3 percentage point underperformance. Compounded over 20 years, that gap results in significantly less wealth. This is why Buffett and most financial economists recommend index funds for most investors.

questions.question: Which index is the most widely used benchmark for measuring global share market performance?
options: Dow Jones Industrial Average — because it is the oldest and most recognised
NASDAQ Composite — because it includes the most technology companies
NZX 50 — because it covers the most economically diverse range of companies
S&P 500 — because it covers 500 large US companies and the US represents the largest share of global market cap
questions.explanation: The S&P 500 is the world's most widely used benchmark because the US represents approximately 60% of total global stock market value. While the Dow Jones is older and more famous in mainstream media, the S&P 500 is considered more representative because it covers 500 companies rather than just 30. Institutional investors globally measure their performance against the S&P 500.

activities.title: Match the Index to Its Market

pairs.zone: 30 major US companies — oldest US index

activities.explanation: Each index has a different composition and purpose. The S&P 500 is the most comprehensive US index. The Dow is more famous but less representative. NASDAQ captures tech-heavy growth companies. The ASX 200 and NZX 50 are the most relevant for NZ investors interested in their home region.

activities.statement: Over long periods of 15+ years, most professional fund managers who actively pick shares beat a simple low-cost S&P 500 index fund.
activities.explanation: False. Research consistently shows approximately 85% to 90% of active fund managers fail to beat their benchmark index over 15 years. This is after fees — and active funds charge significantly more than index funds. The reason is that markets are highly efficient: prices already reflect publicly available information, making consistent outperformance very difficult. This is why index funds are recommended by most economists, Warren Buffett, and financial regulators as the default investment for most individuals.

activities.question: The NZX 50 opens at 12,450 points and closes at 12,637 points. What is the percentage change? Round to two decimal places.
activities.hint: % change = (new value − old value) ÷ old value × 100.
activities.explanation: (12,637 − 12,450) ÷ 12,450 × 100 = 187 ÷ 12,450 × 100 = 1.50%. A 1.5% daily rise is a good day for the NZX. For context, the NZX 50 moves more than 1% in either direction on roughly 50 to 70 days per year. Days with moves over 3% are significant and typically involve major economic news.

activities.title: If You Had $1,000 to Invest Right Now
activities.prompt: You have $1,000 to invest for 10 years. You have three options: (A) put it in a NZX 50 index fund at estimated 9% annual return, (B) give it to an active fund manager at 1.2% fees per year who claims to target 10% returns, (C) leave it in a savings account at 4.5% interest. Use the Rule of 72 to estimate how long each option takes to double. Which would you choose and why?
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — understanding the difference between index funds, active funds and savings accounts is one of the most practically important investment concepts.
